one_line_takeaway: A concise author-perspective survey that organizes LLM watermarking into model vs. content (and hardware-accelerated) branches with two explicit threat models, but contributes taxonomy and open problems rather than any new method or measured result.
key_finding: The survey's qualitative synthesis (Table I) argues that among text-watermarking families, rule-based schemes (e.g., CATER) achieve effectiveness, fidelity, efficiency, and undetectability but are NOT robust (synonym/syntactic edits remove them); inference-time schemes (red/green-list, e.g., KGW) achieve robustness and undetectability but sacrifice fidelity (KGW introduces semantic discrepancies) and efficiency (EXP improves semantics but is slower than KGW); and neural-based schemes (e.g., REMARK-LLM, AWT) are presented as satisfying all five criteria by embedding signatures in the text feature space via lightweight models. Beyond text, the paper's main framing claims are (1) that embedded/quantized-model deployment on edge devices shifts the security model from black-box to white-box and breaks alignment-based watermarks (quantization to INT8/INT4 and pruning can destroy them), motivating weight-level watermarks and TEEs; (2) that domain-specific data (code, medical, chip design) needs functionality/validity-preserving watermarks; and (3) that hardware-software co-design is needed to make watermark insertion/verification scalable.
core_contribution: This is a short survey / position paper (SoK-style, cs.CR) by Ruisi Zhang and Farinaz Koushanfar (UC San Diego), not a new watermarking method. Its own contribution is an organizing taxonomy of the LLM watermarking landscape plus a set of open challenges and future directions. It partitions the space into (1) watermarking the LLM MODEL itself (open-source models via post-training alignment for watermarking/fingerprinting; embedded/quantized models via model-weight watermarking and Trusted Execution Environments), and (2) watermarking LLM-GENERATED CONTENT (natural-language text via rule-based, inference-time, and neural-based schemes; and domain-specific data for code, medical text, and chip/IC physical design). A third axis the authors emphasize is watermarking ACCELERATION through software/hardware co-design (FPGAs, ASICs, AI accelerators, speculative decoding, skip-layer). IMPORTANT for the knowledge graph: the specific algorithms named (KGW, CATER, EXP, AWT, REMARK-LLM, EMMARK, ICMarks, token-specific watermarking, instructional fingerprinting, etc.) are CITED prior work, not this paper's own systems. Several of the surveyed systems (REMARK-LLM [25], EMMARK [21], the GNN-based/ICMarks physical-design watermarks [9],[84], token-specific watermarking [26], the co-design SoK [35]) are the same authors' earlier papers being reviewed here; the survey aggregates and frames them rather than introducing them.
threat_model: The paper articulates two distinct threat models, one per branch of the taxonomy. (A) Model-watermarking threat model (Sec II.C): the adversary has full white-box access to the watermarked LLM parameters and knows the watermark-insertion algorithm, but cannot access the original non-watermarked model and does not know the owner's signatures, the random seeds used to select watermark parameters, or the watermark dataset. Enumerated attacks: parameter-overwriting, re-watermarking, forging (counterfeiting a fake watermark to claim ownership), fine-tuning, and model-pruning/compression. (B) Content-watermarking threat model (Sec III.C): the adversary is a black-box end-user of the LLM cloud API who lacks access to the watermarking models and the original generations, and tries to detect and strip the watermark from generated text while preserving semantics. Enumerated attacks: watermark-detection (statistical/ML), text-edit (random insert/delete/substitute), text-rephrase (e.g., using T5), and re-watermarking (passing the text through another framework such as REMARK-LLM). The overarching goals are IP protection / ownership proof and provenance tracing of AI-generated content (misinformation, deepfakes, plagiarism).
limitations: As the authors themselves flag, current watermarking is not robust to evolving paraphrase/edit/rephrase attacks, offers limited multi-user traceability, and does not yet extend to multimodal (image/audio/video) content; standardization, legal/authentication frameworks, and explainability are open. From a review standpoint the paper's own limitations are more fundamental: it is a brief (~6-page) survey with no empirical validation. Table I is purely qualitative (binary checkmarks) with no metrics, so the strong claim that neural-based methods dominate on all five criteria is asserted rather than measured, and it is drawn substantially from the authors' own systems (REMARK-LLM), creating a self-selection bias. No datasets, no quantitative robustness/fidelity numbers, and no reproducible protocol are provided. Coverage of the fast-moving 2023-2024 literature is illustrative rather than exhaustive.

quantitative_results: No quantitative results of the authors' own: the paper reports no experiments, metrics, or numeric tables.

Table I is a qualitative comparison (checkmark/cross) of three text-watermarking families against five criteria: Rule-based = Effectiveness Y, Fidelity Y, Efficiency Y, Robustness N, Undetectability Y; Inference-time = Effectiveness Y, Fidelity N, Efficiency N, Robustness Y, Undetectability Y; Neural-based = all five Y.
States five watermarking criteria (Effectiveness, Fidelity, Efficiency, Robustness, Undetectability); enumerates 5 attacks on model watermarks and 4 attacks on content watermarks.
Notes embedded-model quantization maps full-precision weights to INT8/INT4 (qualitative, no measured overhead).
baselines_compared: Rule-based text watermarking: CATER [60], invariant-feature multi-bit [61] (cited, qualitatively compared in Table I)
Inference-time (red/green-list) watermarking: KGW / Kirchenbauer et al. [24], EXP / distortion-free [70] (cited)
Neural-based watermarking: REMARK-LLM [25] (authors' own prior work), AWT / Adversarial Watermarking Transformer [69] (cited)
Model/embedded watermarking referents (discussed, not benchmarked): EMMARK [21], weight-quantization watermark [43], instructional fingerprinting [46], DeepSigns [55], TEE-based protection [48]-[50]
Domain-specific referents: code watermarks [57],[71],[72],[76],[77]; medical-data watermarks [73]-[75],[79]; chip/IC physical-design watermarks ICMarks/GNN [9],[84]
